market_temperature_label: A powerful risk-on regime took hold as the S&P 500 posted three consecutive record closes, the VIX plunged 9.1% to 17.48, and the Nasdaq surged 6.84% on the week — all underpinned by the Iran Strait of Hormuz reopening, collapsing oil prices, and blockbuster Wall Street bank earnings.

section1_summary.title: Market Overview
paragraphs: Global equity markets delivered one of the most powerful weekly rallies of 2026, with the S&P 500 surging 4.54% to close at 7,126.06 — entering the 7,000 era for the first time and posting three consecutive record closes. The Nasdaq Composite rocketed 6.84% to 24,468.48, its best weekly performance in months, while extending a 13-session winning streak that echoes the dot-com era. The Dow Jones Industrial Average added 3.19% to close at 49,447.43, tantalizingly close to the 50,000 milestone, while the Russell 2000 outperformed with a 5.56% weekly gain to 2,776.90, signaling broad participation beyond mega-cap tech. European indices joined the rally, with the DAX climbing 3.77% to 24,702.24 and the CAC 40 adding 2.00% to 8,425.13. In Asia, the Nikkei 225 rose 2.73% to 58,475.90, KOSPI soared 5.68%, and China's CSI 300 erased all its Iran-war losses.

The week's dominant narrative was the geopolitical de-escalation in the Middle East. Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz 'completely open' on Friday following a 10-day Israel-Lebanon ceasefire, sending WTI crude plunging 13.17% to $83.85/bbl and Brent 5.06% to $90.38/bbl. The energy selloff ignited a violent sector rotation: Technology surged 8.22% on the week while Energy retreated 3.37%, as falling oil rekindled rate-cut hopes and pushed the 10-year Treasury yield down to 4.246%. Wall Street banks kicked off earnings season with blockbuster results — Goldman Sachs posted record equities trading revenue, JPMorgan beat on every metric with $50.5 billion in revenue and 21% fixed-income trading growth, and Citi logged its highest quarterly trading income since the financial crisis. The AI infrastructure buildout continued unabated, with TSMC raising its 2026 outlook and Microsoft committing $17.4 billion to Nebius Group.

The market's posture is unequivocally risk-on, but the foundation beneath it is more fragile than the price action suggests. By Saturday, Iran had already reversed course and re-closed the Strait of Hormuz after the US refused to lift its naval blockade — a development not yet reflected in Friday's exuberant closes. The ceasefire between the US and Iran expires on April 22, and no permanent peace deal has been finalized despite President Trump's claims of imminent agreement. The VIX dropping to 17.48 indicates options markets are complacent, pricing in a benign resolution. Meanwhile, the dollar weakened 0.56% on the DXY index to 98.10, reflecting a shift away from safe-haven positioning. Gold continued its relentless ascent to $4,857.60/oz — a paradoxical signal suggesting that despite equity euphoria, structural hedging demand remains elevated. For investors, this is a week that rewarded staying invested, but the binary risk around ceasefire expiration means the next seven days could be equally dramatic in either direction.

events.title: Iran Declares Strait of Hormuz Open — Then Reverses
events.body: In the week's pivotal geopolitical development, Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi declared the Strait of Hormuz 'completely open' to commercial shipping on Friday, following a 10-day ceasefire between Israel and Hezbollah in Lebanon brokered by President Trump. The announcement was seen as a breakthrough toward peace, and markets responded with explosive force: WTI crude cratered 13.17% on the week to $83.85/bbl, Brent plunged to $90.38/bbl, and European natural gas fell 10% to around €39/MWh. US equities surged, with the S&P 500 adding 1.20% on Friday alone to close at a fresh record of 7,126.06. However, confusion rapidly emerged. Trump confirmed the US naval blockade of Iranian ports would remain in effect, and Iranian state media affiliated with the Revolutionary Guard described only a limited reopening with IRGC coordination required. By Saturday, Iran had effectively re-closed the strait. An estimated 135 million barrels of oil remain stranded in tankers in the Persian Gulf, and shipping companies including Maersk adopted a 'wait and see' approach, with one shipowner asking: 'Would you be the first penguin off the ice floe to test the water?' The IEA warned recovery of disrupted oil and gas production could take up to two years.

events.title: Wall Street Banks Post Blockbuster Q1 Earnings
events.body: The Q1 2026 earnings season opened with a bang as all major Wall Street banks topped expectations, powered by surging trading revenues amid elevated market volatility. Goldman Sachs reported record equities trading revenue for the second consecutive quarter and a 48% surge in investment banking fees. JPMorgan Chase delivered $50.5 billion in revenue (vs. $49.2 billion expected) and net income of $16.5 billion, with markets revenue hitting a record $11.6 billion — fixed income trading jumped 21% and equity markets revenue rose 17%. Citigroup logged its highest quarterly trading income since the global financial crisis. Credit-loss provisions at JPMorgan came in $500 million below expectations at $2.5 billion. However, JPMorgan lowered its full-year NII guidance from $104.5 billion to $103 billion, and CEO Jamie Dimon flagged 'an increasingly complex set of risks — geopolitical tensions and wars, energy price volatility.' The Financials sector gained 3.27% on the week but underperformed tech, as investors pivoted toward growth names. The results confirmed that the US consumer and corporate borrower remain resilient despite the Iran war shock.

events.title: S&P 500 Enters the 7,000 Era — Nasdaq Posts Longest Win Streak Since 2009
events.body: The S&P 500 broke above 7,000 for the first time in history on Wednesday, April 15, closing at 7,022.95 and proceeding to notch three consecutive record closes through Friday at 7,126.06. The index gained 4.54% on the week — its best weekly performance since May 2025 — and is now up 3.90% year-to-date after fully recouping all losses sustained since the Iran war began on February 28. The Nasdaq Composite posted an even more remarkable 6.84% weekly gain, extending a winning streak to 13 consecutive sessions, the longest since 2009. Retail investors who had been on the sidelines began aggressively chasing the rally, with JPMorgan client flow data showing participation jumping to the 55th percentile from the 10th percentile just days earlier. Technology led all sectors with an 8.22% gain, followed by Consumer Discretionary at 6.66% and KOSPI surging 5.68%. Oracle rallied toward its best weekly gain since 1999, and Intel continued a spectacular 90% year-to-date advance. The rally reflects a market betting on the 'TACO' trade — Trump Always Chickens Out — the belief that economic pain will force policy reversals.

events.title: Netflix Tanks on Weak Guidance and Reed Hastings Board Exit
events.body: Netflix shares plummeted nearly 10% on Friday after the streaming giant reported Q1 earnings that disappointed on the guidance front. The company's projections for second-quarter revenue and operating income came in below analyst targets, and it disclosed that co-founder and chairman Reed Hastings would not seek re-election to the board when his term expires in June, ending a 29-year association with the company he co-founded. Analysts at LightShed Partners said Hastings' exit 'is spooking investors,' particularly as the departure comes less than two months after Netflix's shocking decision to withdraw from the bidding war for Warner Bros. Discovery. Netflix said second-quarter content amortization would be the highest year-over-year in 2026, implying heavier spending ahead. The broader question for the streaming sector is whether Netflix is transitioning from a disruptive growth story into a mature media platform. Despite the selloff, Netflix's year-to-date performance had been solid, and the stock remains far above pandemic-era lows. The move was the largest single-stock detractor from the Communication Services sector, which still managed a 4.52% weekly gain.

events.title: Anthropic's Mythos AI Model Raises Cybersecurity Alarm — White House Engages
events.body: Anthropic's powerful new AI model, Mythos, dominated tech headlines after the company disclosed it could find cybersecurity vulnerabilities faster than any human hacker, raising major concerns about potential misuse. Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei met with White House chief of staff Susie Wiles on Friday to discuss the model's implications, even as Anthropic is simultaneously suing the Pentagon over being branded a 'supply chain risk.' Government agencies were reportedly seeking access to Mythos ahead of the visit. The model has created strain on open-source software maintainers, who are being overwhelmed by AI-generated bug reports faster than they can be fixed. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent noted that the US AI lead over China is only 'three to six months,' and praised Mythos as critical to maintaining that edge. Microsoft and Nvidia are working with Anthropic to detect vulnerabilities within Mythos before wider release. This development accelerates the dual-use AI governance debate and could reshape defense and cybersecurity procurement spending.

section3_blind_spots.title: Blind Spots — What the Market Underestimates

events.title: Hormuz Reversal Risk: Iran Re-Closed the Strait by Saturday
events.body: Markets rallied Friday on Iran's declaration that the Strait of Hormuz was 'completely open,' but by Saturday Tehran had already reversed course, re-imposing restrictions after the US refused to lift its naval blockade. Iranian gunboats reportedly fired on a tanker in the strait on Saturday. The ceasefire expires April 22 — just four days away — with no permanent peace deal in sight. If hostilities resume, oil could rapidly retrace toward the $100-$120 range it reached in March, potentially unwinding the week's entire equity rally. The market is pricing in a smooth resolution with the VIX at 17.48, but the actual probability tree is far more binary. European jet fuel reserves are at critical levels — the IEA warned Europe has 'maybe six weeks' of supply left. Shipping companies including Maersk and Hapag-Lloyd have not resumed normal traffic. The gap between market optimism and operational reality in the strait is the single largest risk-reward asymmetry in global markets today.
events.why_ignored: Markets anchored to Trump's claims of imminent deal and the forward-looking 'TACO trade' narrative, ignoring the mixed signals and Saturday reversal

events.title: Private Credit Stress Lurking Beneath the Surface
events.body: While equity markets celebrate, the $1.7 trillion private credit market is showing signs of strain that have not yet surfaced in public market pricing. Bloomberg's weekend essay highlighted growing anxiety about private credit's opacity and potential for hidden losses, while Bloomberg data showed that AI disruption to software companies — which constitute a large portion of private credit loan books — is accelerating. Earlier this year, investors began worrying about the exposure private debt funds hold in loans tied to software companies at risk of AI disruption. Howard Marks of Oaktree warned that 'people may not have asked enough questions or paid enough attention in the good times.' Big banks including JPMorgan and Morgan Stanley have direct exposure through lending and fund management. CFA Institute flagged 'Private Credit's Verification Problem' this week. The high-yield spread at 2.86% appears tight given these underlying risks.
events.why_ignored: Strong bank earnings and equity euphoria are masking deteriorating private market fundamentals; losses are slow-burn and not yet reflected in quarterly results

events.title: Fed Chair Transition Could Trigger Policy Volatility
events.body: Kevin Warsh's nomination hearing for Federal Reserve chair is now scheduled for April 21, just days before Powell's term expires on May 15. The confirmation process is complicated by Senator Thom Tillis's refusal to vote for any Fed nominee until the DOJ drops its criminal probe into Powell. Democrats are also demanding a delay. With Republicans holding a razor-thin committee majority and Tillis holding firm, there is a meaningful risk that no successor is confirmed by mid-May, creating an unprecedented leadership vacuum at the world's most important central bank. Powell has stated he would continue as 'chair pro tempore,' but Trump has said 'I'll have to fire him.' The dollar has already weakened 0.56% this week to DXY 98.10, partly reflecting uncertainty about Fed continuity. Markets are not adequately pricing the tail risk of a contested Fed leadership transition during a period of elevated oil-driven inflation risk.
events.why_ignored: Warsh hearing is viewed as a procedural formality; market assumes smooth transition despite clear political obstacles

events.title: Europe's Jet Fuel Crisis Could Ground Flights Within Weeks
events.body: The IEA's Fatih Birol warned this week that Europe has 'maybe six weeks' of jet fuel remaining, calling the Hormuz crisis 'the largest energy crisis we have ever faced.' ACI Europe, the trade group for European airports, warned fuel shortages could begin in as little as three weeks. Ryanair flagged it is on track for shortages by June, easyJet has only 70% of summer fuel needs covered, and Lufthansa is cutting long-haul flights and grounding up to 40 planes. Even if Hormuz fully reopens, it will take significant time for supply chains to normalize. European refineries have been declining for decades, and the continent is the biggest recipient of jet fuel that transits the strait. The US is better positioned as the world's largest net exporter of jet fuel, but all major US airlines project billions in added costs. Spirit Airlines may not survive. This energy supply shock has second-order effects on European tourism, GDP, and consumer confidence that equity markets have largely ignored.
events.why_ignored: Equity markets focused on headline Hormuz 'reopening' without modeling the physical logistics of supply chain restoration

us.headline: S&P 500 Breaches 7,100 With Three Consecutive Record Closes; Nasdaq Posts Best Week in Months
us.body: The US equity market delivered an emphatic statement this week. The S&P 500 surged 4.54% to 7,126.06, clearing the psychologically significant 7,000 level for the first time on Wednesday and then extending higher through three consecutive record closes. The index has now fully recovered all losses from the Iran war shock that began February 28, and stands 3.90% higher year-to-date. The Nasdaq Composite's 6.84% weekly gain to 24,468.48 was even more impressive, powered by Technology's 8.22% sector gain as investors rotated aggressively into growth and AI-linked names. The Nasdaq's 13-day winning streak is its longest since 2009. The Russell 2000's 5.56% gain to 2,776.90 demonstrated the rally's breadth — small caps notched their first new closing high since January. Consumer Discretionary gained 6.66% as lower oil prices alleviated consumer spending concerns. On the downside, Energy fell 3.37% as the oil collapse hit producers that had benefited from elevated prices, and chemicals companies LyondellBasell and Dow both fell more than 10% on Friday as the Hormuz opening threatened to normalize supply routes that had advantaged them. Netflix's 9.72% decline was the week's highest-profile single-stock casualty. Retail participation surged mid-week, with JPMorgan data showing buying activity jumping from the 10th to the 55th percentile. Goldman's basket of retail-favored stocks is up 22% since late March.
us.key_driver: Strait of Hormuz reopening and collapsing oil prices rekindled rate-cut expectations and ignited a tech-led risk rally
us.risk: Ceasefire expiration on April 22 without a permanent deal could reverse the entire week's gains; VIX at 17.48 suggests complacency

eu.headline: European Markets Rally on Hormuz Hopes; CAC 40 +2.00%, DAX +3.77%
eu.body: European equities participated in the global risk-on rally but with more caution than their US counterparts, reflecting the continent's acute vulnerability to prolonged energy disruption. The DAX rose 3.77% to 24,702.24, lifted by tech names like Dassault Systèmes (+15.25%), Capgemini (+10.27%), and STMicroelectronics (+9.78%). The CAC 40 advanced 2.00% to 8,425.13, while the EuroStoxx 50 gained 2.22% to 6,057.71 and the FTSE 100 underperformed with just a 0.63% gain to 10,667.60 — reflecting the UK's heavy weighting in energy and mining. The French-led European semiconductor renaissance was a notable theme, with Soitec surging 53.47% on AI-photonics optimism and ITM Power gaining 84.3% on a defense-energy contract with Rheinmetall. However, European flops were revealing: Alstom cratered 28.12% after slashing guidance, Barry Callebaut fell 18.36% on cacao supply disruption, and Kering dropped 8.27% on weak Gucci results. TotalEnergies fell 7.05% as the oil decline hit European energy majors. European leaders met in Paris on Friday to discuss a potential naval mission to secure Hormuz shipping lanes, but Germany and France split on whether the US should participate. The EUR/USD strengthened to 1.1800, reflecting euro resilience as markets priced in de-escalation.
eu.key_driver: Oil price collapse and AI/semiconductor rotation drove gains, particularly in tech-heavy German and French names
eu.risk: Europe's structural jet fuel dependency on Hormuz flows — IEA warned of 6-week supply runway; Ryanair flagged June shortages

asia.headline: Nikkei Rises 2.73% to Near Record; KOSPI Surges 5.68%; China Erases War Losses
asia.body: Asian markets posted strong gains as risk appetite recovered globally, though the rally moderated Friday as investors took profits ahead of the weekend. The Nikkei 225 advanced 2.73% to 58,475.90, approaching its recent record close, driven by AI-linked names including memory chip stocks and Kioxia Holdings. Taiwan's stock market surpassed the UK to become the world's seventh-largest by market capitalization, buoyed by retail investors piling into TSMC, which raised its 2026 revenue outlook after posting a 58% jump in Q1 profits. KOSPI was the week's standout in Asia with a 5.68% gain, now up an extraordinary 43.68% year-to-date, reflecting South Korea's semiconductor exposure and the Samsung/SK Hynix memory chip cycle. China's CSI 300 erased all its Iran war losses, while the Shanghai Composite rose 1.64% to 4,051.43 and the Hang Seng added a modest 1.03% to 26,160.33. China's Q1 GDP came in at 5.0%, above consensus, and house price declines narrowed to their smallest in a year, with Goldman expecting Shanghai and Shenzhen prices to bottom by year-end. Xi Jinping held at least five high-profile diplomatic meetings during the week, positioning China as a stability anchor as Trump's diplomatic relationships frayed. India's Nifty 50 posted its second straight weekly advance as the rupee became Asia's biggest gainer on reports of central bank intervention in FX markets.
asia.key_driver: TSMC earnings upgrade and AI demand resilience; China economic data better than feared; risk-on rotation into EM
asia.risk: Ceasefire expiration risk; China's export outlook darkened by potential prolonged supply chain disruptions; Taiwan strait geopolitical overhang

section5_bonds.title: Bonds & Rates
section5_bonds.macro_context: The US Treasury market rallied sharply this week as plunging oil prices reignited expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts. The 10-year yield fell to 4.246%, its lowest level in a month, while the 2-year yield dropped to 3.78%. The 3-month yield sits at 3.62%, well below the effective fed funds rate of 3.64%, indicating the market sees the current policy rate as the peak. The 2s10s spread widened modestly to 55 basis points, maintaining a normal upward slope that has persisted since the curve un-inverted in late 2025. The 30-year yield settled at 4.885%, reflecting the term premium embedded in longer duration amid fiscal deficit concerns. Breakeven inflation expectations at 2.36% on the 10-year suggest the market believes the oil shock is transitory, a view that will be tested if the ceasefire collapses.

bond_market.title: Global Bond Market
bond_market.why: Treasury yields declined meaningfully on the week as the oil price collapse — WTI down 13.17% — mechanically reduced forward inflation expectations. The 10-year breakeven fell to 2.36%, signaling markets view the energy shock as a supply disruption that will normalize rather than a persistent inflationary impulse. JPMorgan's strong earnings and lower-than-expected credit provisions reinforced the view that the financial system is absorbing the war shock without systemic stress. Treasury Secretary Bessent acknowledged the Fed should cut rates but 'understands' the pause, suggesting the administration is not pushing for immediate action. The 30-year mortgage rate at 6.3% remains elevated, constraining housing activity.
bond_market.implies: The bond market is telling equity investors something nuanced: the rally in duration suggests traders believe the oil shock is peaking, but the 55bp 2s10s spread and 4.885% 30-year yield indicate meaningful term premium. The market is pricing approximately one rate cut in 2026 from the current 3.50-3.75% range, likely deferred until September at the earliest. If the ceasefire holds and oil normalizes toward $75-80, the front end could rally further, steepening the curve. However, if hostilities resume and oil re-spikes, the Fed will be trapped between cutting into an inflationary oil shock and holding amid weakening growth — a classic stagflationary dilemma.
bond_market.strategy: We favor a barbell approach: overweight 2-5 year Treasuries to capture the rally if rate cuts materialize, paired with short positions in 30-year bonds or ultra-long TIPS to hedge the term premium risk from fiscal deficits. Avoid extending duration beyond 10 years given the deficit trajectory and potential for a contested Fed leadership transition.

yield_curves.title: Yield Curves
yield_curves.interpretation: The US yield curve maintains its normal upward slope with a 55bp spread between the 2-year at 3.78% and the 10-year at 4.246%. This is a marked improvement from the deeply inverted curve of 2023-2024 and supports the 'soft landing' narrative. The 3-month at 3.62% sits marginally below the 2-year, suggesting the market expects modest easing over the near term. The steeper back end — 30-year at 4.885% — reflects fiscal term premium as the US borrows more than ever. German bund curves are similarly upward-sloping but at lower absolute levels. Japan's curve is normalizing as the BOJ continues its gradual exit from yield curve control.
yield_curves.why: The curve's normal shape reflects three forces: (1) the Fed at or near peak rates with the next move likely a cut; (2) improving inflation expectations as oil retreats; and (3) fiscal premium in the long end driven by the widening US deficit. The oil price collapse this week pulled intermediate yields lower faster than the long end, mildly steepening the curve.
yield_curves.implies: A normal curve historically supports bank net interest margins and signals economic expansion rather than recession. However, the absolute level of long yields near 5% constrains rate-sensitive sectors like housing and commercial real estate.
yield_curves.strategy: Position for further modest steepening: receive the 5-year, pay the 30-year. Consider TIPS in the 5-7 year segment where breakevens at 2.36% look cheap if the energy disruption proves more persistent than markets expect.

derivatives_and_options.title: Derivatives, Options & Futures
derivatives_and_options.vix_interpretation: The VIX collapsed 9.10% to 17.48, returning to levels not seen since before the Iran war intensified in March. This represents a dramatic compression from the 30+ readings reached during peak conflict anxiety. The move below 18 signals that options markets are pricing in a high probability of continued de-escalation and a smooth resolution to the ceasefire negotiations. However, this level of complacency creates an asymmetric risk profile — the VIX is essentially a coiled spring that could re-explode if the April 22 ceasefire deadline passes without extension or a deal. Realized volatility remains elevated relative to implied, suggesting some hedgers are under-protected.
derivatives_and_options.put_call_interpretation: The estimated put-call ratio near 0.78 reflects moderately bullish positioning, consistent with the aggressive risk-on environment. Put demand has faded as the war hedges established in March are being unwound or rolling off. Volume has concentrated in upside call structures, particularly in Technology and Semiconductor names. However, the S&P 500 options skew — the premium for downside protection — remains above historical averages, indicating that while spot positioning is bullish, sophisticated players are maintaining tail-risk hedges.
derivatives_and_options.skew_interpretation: The CBOE skew index remains moderately elevated around 130, which is above its long-run average of ~120 but well below the 150+ panic levels seen during the initial Iran strikes. This level suggests institutional investors are maintaining meaningful downside tail hedges even as the VIX declines. The divergence between falling VIX and elevated skew is a classic 'hopeful but cautious' configuration — the market is positioned for continued upside but has not abandoned its insurance. This is rational given the binary April 22 deadline.
derivatives_and_options.term_structure_interpretation: The VIX term structure has returned to contango, with near-term implied volatility below deferred months. This is the natural 'calm' configuration and represents a significant shift from the backwardation that prevailed during the March war escalation. The front-month VIX futures settled near 18.5, with the second month around 20.5, implying the market expects slightly elevated volatility but not a spike. The contango structure makes long-volatility positions expensive via futures, but it also means that any disruption to the ceasefire could cause a rapid, violent snap back to backwardation.
derivatives_and_options.futures_positioning: CFTC Commitment of Traders data from the prior reporting period showed asset managers increasing net long equity futures positions across S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 contracts. Leveraged funds have been reducing their hedges as the ceasefire holds. In Treasury futures, positioning has shifted toward net long (duration exposure) in the belly of the curve, reflecting rate-cut bets. In crude oil, the net speculative long position has shrunk dramatically as funds that rode the war premium higher have been actively liquidating. Gold futures continue to see robust speculative longs, with positioning near multi-year highs. The dollar saw a notable increase in short positions from leveraged accounts, consistent with the DXY decline to 98.10.
derivatives_and_options.market_signal_explanation: The derivatives complex is sending a moderately bullish signal. Falling VIX, contango term structure, declining put-call ratios, and reduced crude longs all point to a market that has moved through the war-fear phase and is positioning for normalization. However, the elevated skew and residual tail-hedge demand suggest this is 'cautious bullishness' rather than outright euphoria. The key inflection point is the April 22 ceasefire expiration — if a deal or extension materializes, expect VIX to compress further below 16 and equity upside to continue. If negotiations collapse, the snap-back in volatility could be explosive.
derivatives_and_options.options_strategy: The current environment favors selling elevated skew through put spreads on the S&P 500 (e.g., sell 6900/6700 put spreads for May expiry), funded by purchasing call spreads (7200/7400) to capture continued upside. For those wanting protection, the contango term structure makes calendar spreads attractive: buy May VIX calls, sell April VIX calls. On individual stocks, consider selling elevated implied volatility on oil majors like ExxonMobil, which carry war premiums that will compress on a deal.

key_levels.key_strike: 7,000 put strike has the largest open interest for April/May expiry
$5,000 call strike has massive open interest reflecting upside speculation
1.2000 call options see significant open interest in June expiry

credit_defaults.title: Credit & Default Rates
credit_defaults.macro_view: The US high-yield spread tightened to 2.86% as credit markets followed equities in the risk-on pivot. This level remains remarkably tight by historical standards and represents a significant compression from the 4%+ levels reached briefly during the March war panic. Investment-grade spreads have similarly compressed. The strong bank earnings — particularly JPMorgan's lower-than-expected credit-loss provisions at $2.5 billion — provided fundamental support. However, the tightness of credit spreads is increasingly at odds with underlying risks in private credit, where opacity around loan valuations in AI-disrupted software companies is growing.
credit_defaults.why: Credit markets are being supported by three factors: (1) the strong bank earnings cycle demonstrating consumer and corporate credit quality remains solid; (2) the risk-on rally compressing risk premia across all asset classes; and (3) the expectation that the Fed's next move is a cut, which supports the carry trade in high-yield bonds. JPMorgan reported card spending growth of 9% YoY with delinquency rates improving, while credit-loss provisions came in well below expectations.
credit_defaults.implies: At 2.86%, the US HY spread offers minimal compensation for tail risks including a potential war resumption, private credit contagion, or a delayed Fed pivot. Credit investors are being paid to absorb risk that is arguably mispriced. The 30-year mortgage at 6.3% is constraining housing, but consumer credit metrics remain healthy for now.
credit_defaults.strategy: Reduce high-yield credit exposure and rotate toward investment-grade corporates and structured credit in the 3-5 year maturity bucket. The risk-reward in HY at sub-300bp spreads is unfavorable given the geopolitical binary. Selectively add exposure to energy-sector credit, where issuers have de-risked balance sheets and benefit from elevated cash flows even as oil normalizes toward $85-90.

section6_forex.title: Foreign Exchange Markets

dollar_index.interpretation: The DXY fell 0.56% to 98.10, extending its retreat from the war-driven haven highs above 103 reached in March. The breakdown below 99 is technically significant, representing the first sustained move below this level since early 2025. Wall Street banks including Deutsche Bank and Wells Fargo have turned bearish on the dollar, arguing that the war-driven haven rally is over and investors should embrace short-dollar trades. State Street data shows global investors have boosted dollar hedging ratios to a two-year high. The dollar weakness is consistent with the broader risk-on narrative — as geopolitical risk premiums deflate, capital is flowing out of the dollar and into risk assets, EM currencies, and commodity-linked FX.

section6_forex.narrative: The foreign exchange market this week was characterized by broad dollar weakness and a rotation toward risk-sensitive and commodity-linked currencies. EUR/USD advanced 0.65% to 1.1800, supported by the oil price decline that reduces Europe's energy import bill and by improved global risk appetite. GBP/USD rose 0.64% to 1.3500 despite UK political turbulence around PM Starmer and the Mandelson scandal. USD/JPY edged lower to 158.58, with the yen finding modest support from its safe-haven status even in a risk-on week — suggesting the BOJ's tightening path provides structural yen support. The Swiss franc was the week's top performer in the G10, with USD/CHF dropping 1.45% to 0.7800 as Switzerland's traditional safe-haven appeal attracted flows. AUD/USD surged 2.11% to 0.7200, benefiting from commodity price support and China's better-than-expected Q1 GDP. In EM, the Indian rupee became Asia's biggest gainer on reports the RBI directed state-owned refiners to route dollar purchases through a special facility. USD/BRL and USD/MXN both declined, reflecting broad EM strength. The overall FX picture supports the risk-on thesis but the dollar's weakening trend could become self-reinforcing if the ceasefire holds.

pairs.analysis: EUR/USD broke decisively above 1.18 this week, its highest level in months, as falling oil prices reduce Europe's energy import costs and geopolitical risk premiums deflate. The pair benefited from Xi Jinping's diplomatic charm offensive, which positioned China as a stability anchor and implicitly weakened the dollar's unique haven premium. European PMI flash readings next week will test whether the manufacturing recovery narrative can sustain this level. Spain and Brazil signed economic agreements at a Barcelona summit, signaling growing non-dollar trade corridors.

Cable advanced 0.64% to 1.3500 despite PM Starmer facing fresh calls for resignation over the Mandelson-Epstein affair. UK GDP data for February beat expectations at 0.5% MoM, though Goldman Sachs noted this pre-dates the war's impact. The UK co-hosted a Hormuz security summit in Paris with France. Next week's UK CPI and PMI releases will be critical for BOE rate expectations, with inflation expected to accelerate to 3.3% YoY from 3.0%.

USD/JPY edged 0.33% lower to 158.58, a modest move relative to the dollar's broader weakness. The yen is caught between two forces: risk-on sentiment that typically weakens the yen, and BOJ policy normalization that provides structural support. Japan's trade balance data next week and Friday's CPI release will inform expectations for further BOJ tightening. The Nikkei's strong performance suggests foreign flows into Japan are partially offsetting yen strength.

The Swiss franc was the G10's top performer, with USD/CHF dropping 1.45% to 0.7800. The franc is benefiting from its traditional safe-haven appeal at a time when the dollar's haven premium is eroding. SNB rate expectations remain accommodative, but the franc's strength suggests capital is rotating from dollar cash into European safe havens as a hedge against US policy unpredictability.

AUD/USD surged 2.11% to 0.7200, its best weekly performance in months. The Aussie benefited from China's above-consensus Q1 GDP of 5.0%, the rally in copper (+3.97%), and broad risk-on sentiment. Australia announced a multibillion-dollar deal to purchase Japanese frigates, deepening defense ties. The pair's rally above 0.72 puts it at levels not seen since late 2024 and signals macro optimism about Asia-Pacific growth.

USD/CNY drifted 0.22% lower to 6.8200, reflecting modest yuan strength as China's Q1 data exceeded expectations and Xi Jinping's diplomatic positioning strengthened. The PBOC is expected to release loan prime rates on Monday. Goldman expects Shanghai and Shenzhen property prices to bottom by year-end, which if realized would be a significant positive for CNY.

USD/BRL fell 0.31% to 4.9900, with the real benefiting from EM risk-on flows and Brazil's role in Spain's anti-Trump diplomatic coalition at the Barcelona summit. President Lula met with Sánchez to sign trade agreements, and Brazil's positioning as a key agricultural exporter provides structural support during global supply chain disruptions.

USD/MXN retreated 0.45% to 17.2900, reflecting improved EM sentiment and the peso's high-carry appeal. Mexico's leaders attended the Barcelona summit, signaling a coordinated Latin American diplomatic stance. The peso's resilience despite oil price declines suggests the currency is being driven more by carry trade dynamics and nearshoring investment flows than by petroleum revenue.

energy.narrative: Energy markets experienced their most dramatic reversal of the year as Iran's declaration of the Strait of Hormuz being 'completely open' triggered a massive selloff in crude oil. WTI crashed 13.17% to $83.85/bbl, its steepest weekly decline since the pandemic, while Brent plunged to $90.38/bbl — falling below $90 for the first time in over a month. Heating oil collapsed 9.68% to $3.40/gal. The selloff accelerated after Axios reported the US was considering unfreezing $20 billion of Iranian funds in exchange for enriched uranium stockpiles. However, the price action may have overshot the fundamental reality: by Saturday, Iran had reversed its Hormuz opening, shipping companies had not resumed normal traffic, and an estimated 172 million barrels of crude remained stranded on laden tankers in the Persian Gulf. The IEA warned it could take two years to fully recover disrupted production. Natural gas was the outlier, rising 0.98% to $2.67/MMBtu, as the supply/demand fundamentals in US gas remain structurally different from oil. Gasoline RBOB fell a modest 1.07% to $3.00/gal — notably less than crude, reflecting the fact that US refining capacity is domestically sufficient.

items.name: WTI Crude
items.analysis: WTI's 13.17% weekly decline to $83.85 was one of the sharpest moves in recent memory, driven by the Hormuz reopening headline. The move brought prices back to levels seen in the early days of the war. However, the Friday closing price does not reflect Saturday's Iranian reversal. We expect significant intraday volatility early next week as markets digest the latest developments.

items.name: Brent Crude
items.analysis: Brent's 5.06% weekly decline to $90.38 was less extreme than WTI, reflecting the international benchmark's tighter fundamentals. The Brent-WTI spread narrowed as the Hormuz opening disproportionately benefits US benchmark pricing. Brent remains above the pre-war level of approximately $75, indicating the market still embeds a meaningful geopolitical premium.

items.name: Natural Gas
items.analysis: Natural gas bucked the energy selloff trend, rising 0.98% to $2.67/MMBtu. US gas supply/demand fundamentals are largely insulated from Hormuz disruptions, and the market is transitioning toward summer cooling demand. European benchmark gas did fall 10% to ~€39/MWh on the Hormuz headline.

items.name: Heating Oil
items.analysis: Heating oil plunged 9.68% to $3.40/gal, tracking the crude selloff closely. The diesel/heating oil complex had been among the hardest hit by the Hormuz disruption due to European refineries' dependence on Middle Eastern feedstock. The decline provides relief to transportation and logistics operators.

items.name: Gasoline RBOB
items.analysis: RBOB gasoline fell a relatively modest 1.07% to $3.00/gal, as US refining capacity is largely self-sufficient. Retail pump prices remain elevated and 'sting more in cities where driving is the only option,' as Bloomberg noted. Trump pivoted back to affordability messaging with an eye on midterms.

metals.narrative: Precious and industrial metals diverged this week. Gold continued its relentless ascent, gaining 2.01% to $4,857.60/oz despite the risk-on equity environment — a paradoxical signal reflecting ongoing structural demand for portfolio hedges, central bank purchases, and dollar weakness. Silver outperformed dramatically, surging 7.09% to $81.74/oz, reflecting both its precious-metal attributes and its industrial demand profile in an improving global growth scenario. Copper, the global bellwether for industrial activity, rose 3.97% to $6.10/lb, supported by China's strong Q1 GDP data and the expectation that post-war reconstruction and energy transition spending will boost demand. Aluminum leapt 4.87% to $3,596.75/t on supply disruption concerns and restocking demand. Platinum and palladium both gained approximately 3.7-4.1%, buoyed by the broader metals rally and modest auto-sector demand recovery. The metals complex as a whole is sending a message of reflation and structural demand that transcends the week's geopolitical headlines.

items.name: Gold
items.analysis: Gold's 2.01% rise to $4,857.60 in a strong risk-on week underscores the structural nature of its bid. Central bank purchasing, de-dollarization flows, and inflation hedging continue to support prices. WSJ's weekend feature explored 'the seductive power of gold' as a thematic trend. The fact that gold rallied alongside equities suggests it is trading as a monetary alternative rather than a fear hedge.

items.name: Silver
items.analysis: Silver's 7.09% surge to $81.74/oz was the week's top performer among precious metals, benefiting from its dual role as a monetary metal and industrial commodity. The gold-silver ratio compressed, a historically bullish signal for risk assets. Solar panel manufacturing demand continues to support physical silver fundamentals.

items.name: Copper
items.analysis: Copper advanced 3.97% to $6.10/lb, a level that reflects optimism about Chinese construction demand, energy transition capex, and post-war reconstruction spending. China's above-consensus GDP data provided fundamental support. The red metal remains a key barometer for global economic health.

items.name: Platinum
items.analysis: Platinum gained 3.68% to $2,124.50/oz, supported by the broad metals rally and improving auto-sector demand expectations as lower fuel costs would support consumer spending on vehicles. Supply constraints in South Africa continue to provide a floor.

items.name: Palladium
items.analysis: Palladium rose 4.08% to $1,590.40/oz, tracking the broader PGM complex higher. The metal remains structurally challenged by the EV transition reducing catalytic converter demand, but short-term auto production recovery and risk-on flows provided support.

items.name: Aluminum
items.analysis: Aluminum surged 4.87% to $3,596.75/t, one of the week's top base metal performers. The rally reflects restocking demand after war-related supply disruptions, particularly as Middle Eastern smelters are major global producers. China's industrial recovery and defense-related demand provide additional support.

agricultural.narrative: Agricultural commodities presented a mixed picture this week, caught between the deflationary impulse from falling oil prices and persistent supply chain concerns from the Hormuz disruption. Wheat gained 3.55% to 591.25¢/bu, supported by disruptions to Russian Black Sea exports (Ukraine stepped up drone attacks on Baltic ports) and concerns about global fertilizer supply — prices could average 15-20% higher in H1 2026 if the crisis continues. Corn rose 1.76% to 448.75¢/bu on similar supply concerns. Cotton surged 5.71% to 77.40¢/lb, the week's top agricultural gainer, as lower oil prices improved textile manufacturing economics while demand from reopening Asian economies supported prices. Coffee fell 3.60% to 289.30¢/lb as the risk-off premium unwound, while sugar declined 3.20% to 13.31¢/lb. Soybeans edged lower by 0.72% to 1,167.25¢/bu. The IMF warned this week that 20 million more people in Africa could face food insecurity from the war's disruption of fertilizer supplies, making the agricultural sector a critical humanitarian as well as market concern.

items.name: Wheat
items.analysis: Wheat's 3.55% gain reflects the tension between lower oil (reducing input costs) and disrupted Russian exports. Ukraine's drone attacks on Russian Baltic ports — Primorsk and Ust-Luga — are disrupting grain shipping infrastructure. Fertilizer supply constraints from the Hormuz closure add to production cost concerns for the upcoming harvest.
drivers: Russian export disruptions from Ukraine drone attacks

items.name: Corn
items.analysis: Corn gained 1.76% to 448.75¢/bu, tracking wheat higher on supply concerns. US planting season is underway with fertilizer costs elevated due to Hormuz disruptions. The IMF's warning about 20 million additional people facing food insecurity added urgency to the agricultural supply narrative.

items.name: Soybeans
items.analysis: Soybeans dipped 0.72% to 1,167.25¢/bu, underperforming other grains. Chinese demand remains a question mark despite strong Q1 GDP, and South American supply from Brazil's record harvest is providing ample global stocks. The US-China relationship remains complex but less hostile than during the tariff wars.
drivers: Favorable weather in key growing regions

items.name: Coffee
items.analysis: Coffee retreated 3.60% to 289.30¢/lb as the risk-off war premium unwound. Shipping disruptions from the Hormuz closure had elevated coffee prices due to longer transit routes for key producers. The reopening headline, even if premature, allowed speculative longs to take profits.

items.name: Sugar
items.analysis: Sugar declined 3.20% to 13.31¢/lb, pressured by the broader commodity selloff and improved supply outlook from Brazil. The fall in oil prices reduces the economics of sugar-to-ethanol diversion in Brazil, which should increase sugar available for export.

items.name: Cotton
items.analysis: Cotton surged 5.71% to 77.40¢/lb, the week's top agricultural performer. Lower oil prices reduce synthetic fiber competitiveness relative to natural cotton, while improving Asian demand and supply constraints from weather-related disruptions in key producing regions supported the rally.
drivers: Lower oil favors cotton over synthetics

section8_synthesis.title: Strategic Synthesis
section8_synthesis.regime_description: The global macro regime is transitioning from wartime risk-off to cautious risk-on, but the transition is far from complete. The S&P 500 at all-time highs, VIX at 17.48, and collapsing oil prices all point to a market that has aggressively priced in peace. However, Iran's Saturday reversal on Hormuz, the April 22 ceasefire deadline, and the Fed leadership transition create significant event risk that keeps us from classifying this as a fully established risk-on or Goldilocks regime. The 10-year yield at 4.246% and gold at $4,857 suggest fixed income and commodity markets are less sanguine than equities about the durability of this calm.
section8_synthesis.global_view: This was a landmark week for global markets — the S&P 500 entered the 7,000 era, the Nasdaq posted its longest winning streak since 2009, and the Strait of Hormuz was declared open for the first time since the Iran war began in late February. The rally was global in scope: KOSPI surged 5.68%, DAX gained 3.77%, Nikkei rose 2.73%, and China's CSI 300 erased all war-related losses after Q1 GDP beat at 5.0%. Wall Street banks delivered blockbuster Q1 results — Goldman's record equities trading, JPMorgan's $50.5 billion revenue and 21% fixed-income growth, Citi's best trading quarter since the GFC — confirming that elevated volatility is a windfall for market-makers even as it creates stress elsewhere. TSMC's raised 2026 outlook validated AI demand resilience, and Oracle was on track for its best week since 1999. However, the euphoria must be tempered by the fragility of its foundation. The ceasefire expires April 22 — four days from now — and no permanent deal exists despite Trump's optimistic claims. By Saturday, Iran had reversed its Hormuz opening. The IEA warned that even with full reopening, oil and gas recovery could take two years. Europe faces a potential jet fuel crisis within six weeks. The dollar weakened to DXY 98.10 as Wall Street turned bearish on the greenback, while gold's continued ascent to $4,857.60 alongside equity records signals that structural hedging demand is not fading. The 10-year breakeven at 2.36% appears complacent about inflation persistence if oil re-spikes. Kevin Warsh's Fed nomination hearing on April 21 introduces central bank uncertainty just as Powell's term expires May 15. In credit, HY spreads at 286bp look tight given the private credit anxiety highlighted by Bloomberg's analysis and Oaktree's Howard Marks. The retail investor surge — from 10th to 55th percentile participation per JPMorgan — echoes the late stages of prior risk-on episodes. We characterize this market as 'priced for perfection on a binary event' — the optimal configuration for those who are right about peace, and a dangerous one for those who are wrong.
section8_synthesis.macro_thesis: Our base case (55% probability) is that the US-Iran ceasefire is extended for two additional weeks, allowing talks to continue and the Hormuz reopening to gradually normalize. In this scenario, the S&P 500 can consolidate above 7,000, oil drifts toward $80-85 on WTI, and the 10-year yield settles around 4.1-4.2% as rate-cut expectations firm. The alternative scenario (35% probability) involves a ceasefire collapse, with oil spiking back toward $100-110 and equities giving back 5-8% in days. A tail scenario (10% probability) involves a broader escalation that sends oil toward $130+ and triggers a global recession. The key variables to monitor are: (1) the April 22 ceasefire deadline; (2) the Warsh hearing on April 21; (3) US retail sales data on Monday; (4) flash PMIs on Wednesday/Thursday. Structurally, the AI capex cycle remains the most powerful secular tailwind — TSMC's raised outlook, Nvidia's ecosystem investments, Anthropic's Mythos, and Microsoft's $17.4 billion Nebius commitment all reinforce this. We expect Technology to continue outperforming in any scenario short of full-blown recession.
section8_synthesis.upcoming_events: Next week is one of the most event-rich of 2026. The US-Iran ceasefire expires on Tuesday, April 22, making Monday and Tuesday's geopolitical headlines the week's dominant driver. Kevin Warsh's Fed chair nomination hearing is scheduled for Monday, April 21, at 10 AM ET — his testimony on monetary policy, Fed independence, and rate outlook will be market-moving. US retail sales data on Monday (consensus +1.3% MoM) will provide the first hard data on consumer resilience during the war period. Flash PMIs on Wednesday for Germany, UK, and the US will gauge whether the oil shock is filtering into economic activity. UK CPI on Tuesday (expected 3.3% vs. 3.0%) could reset BOE expectations. Japan CPI on Thursday and German Ifo on Thursday round out the macro picture. Earnings season continues with UnitedHealth Group, Blackstone, Procter & Gamble, Intel (April 23), Tesla, and GE Vernova headlining.

strategy.recommendation: Reduce equity overweight from maximum to modest overweight; build cash buffer to 10-15% for potential ceasefire-related volatility
strategy.rationale: With the S&P 500 at all-time highs and VIX at 17.48, the market is priced for a smooth resolution of the Iran conflict. The April 22 ceasefire deadline creates a binary risk that is not adequately compensated at current positioning levels. By reducing equity exposure from maximum bullish to a modest overweight, we maintain upside participation if the ceasefire extends while preserving capital to deploy on a pullback. The cash buffer allows opportunistic buying if oil re-spikes and equities correct 3-5%.

strategy.recommendation: Purchase S&P 500 May put spreads (6900/6700) funded by selling call spreads (7300/7500); establish long VIX May calls
strategy.rationale: The VIX term structure in contango makes near-term options relatively cheap. A 6900/6700 put spread costs approximately 15-20bp of portfolio value and would provide 2% of downside protection in a war resumption scenario. The 7300/7500 call spread sale partially funds the hedge and caps upside at levels the market would only reach in a full peace scenario. Long VIX May calls provide a convex hedge against the specific binary risk of the April 22 ceasefire deadline. The current VIX at 17.48 provides attractive entry for volatility longs.

strategy.recommendation: Overweight European and Asian semiconductor stocks; add to TSMC, ASML, and Soitec on any dip
strategy.rationale: The AI infrastructure capex cycle is the most powerful secular trend in global markets and is now being validated by Q1 earnings. TSMC raised its 2026 revenue growth forecast to 30%+ with 'extremely robust' AI demand. ASML upgraded its 2026 sales guidance to €36-40 billion. European names like Soitec (+53.47% this week) and STMicroelectronics offer cheaper entry points than US mega-cap tech with similar AI exposure. The sector will outperform whether the ceasefire holds or collapses, as AI investment is not contingent on Middle Eastern geopolitics.

strategy.recommendation: Short US Energy sector (XLE) vs. long Technology sector (XLK) as a pair trade
strategy.rationale: The energy sector fell 3.37% this week as oil collapsed, while technology surged 8.22%. This rotation has further to run in our base case of ceasefire extension and oil normalization. Energy stocks like LyondellBasell and Dow were the week's worst S&P 500 performers, and the sector's year-to-date outperformance of +21.31% is vulnerable to mean-reversion if oil stabilizes in the $80-90 range. The XLE/XLK pair trade is neutral to broader market direction and captures the ongoing rotation from old economy to AI economy. It also provides a natural hedge: if the ceasefire collapses and oil spikes, the short XLE leg would underperform but the fundamental energy company tailwind could offset losses.

strategy.recommendation: Set hard stop-losses on all equity positions at 3% below current levels; reduce high-yield credit exposure to underweight; maintain gold allocation at 5-7% of portfolio
strategy.rationale: The binary nature of the April 22 ceasefire deadline requires disciplined risk management. A 3% stop on equity positions limits drawdown to manageable levels while allowing normal intra-week volatility. High-yield spreads at 286bp offer insufficient compensation for ceasefire tail risk and private credit contagion potential. Gold at $4,857 continues to serve as the ultimate portfolio insurance — its ability to rally alongside equities this week demonstrates its structural appeal beyond simple fear hedging. Maintaining a 5-7% gold allocation provides diversification that performs in both our base and alternative scenarios.

events.context: Canada's CPI will be closely watched as PM Carney prepares a fiscal update. The IMF lauded Canada's fiscal position as strongest in the G7. Higher energy costs from the Hormuz crisis may push headline CPI higher.
UK labor market data will inform BOE policy expectations. The UK economy showed surprise strength in February but the war's impact on Q2 is the key uncertainty. Starmer faces political headwinds.
ZEW sentiment is expected to deteriorate sharply to -10.0 from -0.5, reflecting the Iran war's impact on German business confidence. A worse-than-expected reading could test the DAX's recent rally.

Retail sales expected to jump 1.3% MoM, the strongest reading in months. Strong bank earnings showed consumer spending grew faster than a year ago. This release is critical for confirming the 'resilient consumer' thesis that underpins the equity rally.
UK CPI expected to accelerate to 3.3% from 3.0%, driven by energy price pass-through from the Hormuz crisis. A hot reading would reduce BOE rate cut expectations and pressure gilts.

German manufacturing PMI expected to cool from 52.2 to 51.2, still in expansion territory but reflecting the drag from energy disruptions. The reading will test whether European industry is absorbing the oil shock.

UK manufacturing PMI forecast to slip below 50 into contraction at 49.5, a significant deterioration that would confirm the war's impact on British industry. Combined with elevated CPI, this could create a stagflationary narrative for the UK.

Japan CPI will inform BOJ tightening expectations. The Nikkei is near record highs and the BOJ has been gradually normalizing policy. An upside surprise could strengthen the yen and impact carry trades.

Ifo business climate expected to fall from 86.4 to 84.8, its lowest in months. German industry is a key bellwether for Eurozone growth, and deterioration here would challenge the DAX's recent strength.

section10_earnings_calendar.title: Earnings Calendar — Next Week
section10_earnings_calendar.note: Sources: Finnhub, Wall Street Journal, Bloomberg

earnings.context: The insurance bellwether faces scrutiny over 'longer-term structural challenges' at the heart of its business, according to WSJ. Despite a recent Medicare rate hike that sent the stock up 9% in a single day, investors betting on a return to historical fat margins may be disappointed. Management commentary on medical cost trends is critical.

Blackstone's results will provide the most direct read on private credit and alternative asset market health. With Bloomberg and CFA Institute both flagging private credit stress, Blackstone's commentary on fund valuations, deployment pace, and realized losses will be closely parsed by credit markets.

Tesla reports amid headlines about a six-seater Model Y variant for India and Elon Musk's ambitious Terafab chip manufacturing plant. The company's auto deliveries have been volatile, and rising energy costs have pressured EV economics. Musk's political activities continue to polarize investors and consumers.

TXN is a bellwether for the analog semiconductor cycle. Its results will indicate whether industrial and automotive chip demand is recovering from the inventory correction. The company's massive capex in US fabs makes it relevant to the CHIPS Act manufacturing narrative.

Intel reports its first quarter under the Nvidia partnership framework, with shares up 90% YTD following the $5 billion Nvidia investment and US government backing. Investors will focus on revenue guidance, 18A chip yield progress, and commentary on foundry customer pipeline. CEO Lip-Bu Tan's execution is under the microscope.

P&G's results will gauge global consumer demand and input cost pressures. The company's pricing power in a rising energy cost environment is key. After PepsiCo showed strong results from price cuts, P&G's strategy on volume vs. pricing will set the tone for staples.

GE Vernova provides critical insight into the energy transition capex cycle. As the war disrupts traditional energy supplies, investment in renewable infrastructure and grid resilience takes on greater urgency. Orders and backlog commentary will signal whether the energy transition is accelerating.

AmEx provides a window into premium consumer spending trends. With JPMorgan reporting 9% card spending growth and lower delinquencies, AmEx should confirm affluent consumer resilience. Travel and entertainment spending commentary will also reflect the oil-shock's impact on travel demand.

Capital One's results will provide the most granular read on subprime consumer credit health. Card delinquency trends and provisioning guidance are the key metrics. The company's pending Discover acquisition adds strategic complexity.
